steps: $\mathbb{R}P^1$ is the quotient $S^1/\{\pm 1\}$, identifying antipodal points on the circle.
The map $\theta \mapsto 2\theta$ sends $[0, \pi]/(0\sim\pi)$ homeomorphically to $S^1$. Hence $\mathbb{R}P^1 \cong S^1$.

Alternatively, the double cover $S^1 \to \mathbb{R}P^1$ exhibits $\mathbb{R}P^1$ as the base of a 2-sheeted covering, but the total space $S^1$ and the base $\mathbb{R}P^1$ are both homeomorphic to $S^1$.
Contrast with $\mathbb{R}P^2$, where the identification creates torsion in $H_1$. The extra dimension in $\mathbb{R}P^2$ is responsible.
